bytes.topic: tRPC — end-to-end type safety, procedure types, React Query integration
bytes.title: tRPC — End-to-End Type Safety, Procedure Types, and React Query Integration
bytes.body:
tRPC eliminates the REST/GraphQL schema layer entirely — your router IS the contract. Define procedures on the server using `publicProcedure.input(z.object({...})).query()` or `.mutation()`, and TypeScript propagates those exact types to the client with zero codegen.

Three procedure types exist: `query` (GET-like, cached), `mutation` (write operations), and `subscription` (WebSocket streams via `observable()`). Each accepts a Zod schema for input validation that doubles as your runtime guard and static type source.

The React Query integration is first-class: `trpc.post.byId.useQuery({ id: 1 })` returns a fully-typed `UseQueryResult` — no manual typing, no `as` casts. Mutations use `trpc.post.create.useMutation()` with `onSuccess` receiving the inferred return type. Under the hood, `@trpc/react-query` wraps TanStack Query, so `queryKey` management, invalidation via `utils.post.byId.invalidate()`, and optimistic updates all work natively. Procedure input errors surface as typed `TRPCClientError` with the Zod issue shape intact, letting you map field errors precisely in forms.

bytes.topic: WebSockets — connection lifecycle, heartbeat, reconnection, rooms
bytes.title: tRPC WebSockets — Connection Lifecycle, Heartbeat, Reconnection, and Rooms
bytes.body:
tRPC's WebSocket support lives in `@trpc/server/adapters/ws` on the server and `createWSClient` on the client. The server uses `applyWSSHandler({ wss, router, createContext })`, which wires the raw `ws.Server` into tRPC's subscription machinery. Every `subscription` procedure returns an `observable<T, Error>`, and tRPC manages the open/message/close lifecycle internally — you don't touch raw WebSocket events.

For reconnection, `createWSClient` accepts a `retryDelay` callback: `(attemptIndex) => Math.min(1000 * 2 ** attemptIndex, 30_000)`. tRPC replays pending subscriptions automatically after reconnect, so clients re-enter any active observable without extra logic.

Heartbeats are controlled via `WebSocketServer`'s `clientTracking` + a ping interval you set on the raw `wss`. tRPC doesn't abstract this — set `wss.options.perMessageDeflate` and fire `ws.ping()` on an interval, terminating clients that don't pong.

Rooms are modeled as filtered subscriptions. Pass a `roomId` as subscription input, and use an in-process `EventEmitter` keyed by room. Emit on the correct key; each subscriber's observable only receives matching events. No separate room protocol needed.

bytes.topic: REST — resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, idempotency
bytes.title: tRPC vs REST — Verbs, Status Codes, and Idempotency Without the HTTP Ceremony
bytes.body:
tRPC replaces HTTP's verb/resource model with a typed procedure graph. Your "resource naming" lives in router composition — `appRouter.user.profile` instead of `/users/{id}/profile`. Queries map to GET semantics: they're idempotent by convention, safe to retry, and tRPC's `httpBatchLink` sends them as actual GET requests when inputs are small enough. Mutations always POST — regardless of whether the operation is an update or delete.

tRPC doesn't expose raw HTTP status codes to clients. It surfaces `TRPCError` codes (`NOT_FOUND`, `UNAUTHORIZED`, `CONFLICT`, `BAD_REQUEST`) that the HTTP adapter translates to 404, 401, 409, 400. Throw the right code; the transport layer owns the status number.

Idempotency requires explicit design in mutations. Unlike REST where PUT carries an idempotency guarantee by spec, tRPC mutations have no such contract. Pass an `idempotencyKey` in your input schema, check for an existing record before writing, and return the cached result on replay. Your caller's inferred return type already matches — no extra plumbing needed.

bytes.topic: GraphQL — schema design, resolvers, N+1 with DataLoader, subscriptions
bytes.title: tRPC Subscriptions — Real-Time Without GraphQL's Schema Overhead
bytes.body:
tRPC's subscription procedures use observables over WebSockets, giving you GraphQL-subscription semantics with zero schema boilerplate. Define one with `.subscription()` returning an `observable<T>` — tRPC's client auto-reconnects and `useSubscription` gives you `data`, `error`, and `status` typed end-to-end.

For the N+1 equivalent: tRPC's HTTP batching (on by default) coalesces parallel `useQuery` calls into a single `?batch=1` request. Unlike DataLoader's per-resolver deduplication, tRPC batches at the transport layer — no wiring, no context injection. Disable per-procedure with `httpBatchLink` options if a query must not be batched.

Router composition replaces GraphQL schema stitching: nest routers via `router({ chat: chatRouter, user: userRouter })` to build typed sub-graphs. Subscriptions live in the same router, share the same `ctx`, and type-check through to the client. There's no SDL, no resolver map, no codegen step. The TypeScript inference chain — from `observable<T>` on the server to `data: T` on the client — is the schema.

bytes.topic: gRPC — protobuf schemas, streaming types, when to use over REST
bytes.title: tRPC vs gRPC — When Protobuf and Native Streaming Beat TypeScript RPC
bytes.body:
tRPC's type safety comes from TypeScript inference — no `.proto` files, no codegen. gRPC flips this: protobuf schemas are the source of truth, generating typed stubs for Go, Rust, Java, and TypeScript alike. That polyglot boundary is gRPC's primary advantage over tRPC.

gRPC defines four streaming modes: unary (equivalent to tRPC queries/mutations), server streaming, client streaming, and bidirectional. tRPC's `.subscription()` approximates server streaming via WebSockets using `observable<T>`, but exposes no API surface for client-streaming or bidirectional flows. If a client needs to push a stream of audio chunks while simultaneously receiving transcription back, gRPC is the only clean fit — tRPC's subscription model simply can't express it.

Protobuf serialization is also 3–10x more compact than tRPC's JSON-over-HTTP payload — material at high message throughput or on constrained networks.

Stick with tRPC when your stack is TypeScript end-to-end and zero-codegen type safety is the priority. Reach for gRPC when you have non-TS consumers, need binary wire efficiency, or require client-streaming and bidirectional patterns that tRPC's `observable` abstraction cannot model.

bytes.topic: OpenAPI — spec-first vs code-first, generating clients, documentation
bytes.title: tRPC + OpenAPI — Going Code-First Without Locking Out REST Consumers
bytes.body:
tRPC is inherently code-first — your TypeScript types are the contract, not a YAML file. But when mobile teams or third-party integrations demand OpenAPI, `trpc-openapi` bridges the gap without abandoning type safety.

Annotate any procedure with `.meta({ openapi: { method: 'GET', path: '/users/{id}' } })`, then call `generateOpenApiDocument(appRouter, { title, version, baseUrl })` to produce a valid OpenAPI 3.0 spec derived directly from the router. The router stays the source of truth — the spec is a build artifact, never hand-edited.

For client generation, pipe that spec into `openapi-typescript` or `swagger-codegen`. External consumers hit REST-compatible endpoints via `createOpenApiNextHandler`, while internal Next.js callers still use `createTRPCProxyClient` for full type inference and batching.

Two constraints to know upfront: `trpc-openapi` requires Zod on both `.input()` and `.output()` — no raw-type procedures — and OpenAPI routes lose tRPC's request batching. Neither affects non-annotated procedures, so adoption is incremental.

bytes.topic: Authentication — OAuth2, JWT in headers, API keys, mTLS
bytes.title: tRPC Authentication — JWT, API Keys, and mTLS via Context and Middleware
bytes.body:
tRPC authentication lives entirely in two places: `createContext` and procedure middleware. Never do auth inside a procedure directly.

In `createContext`, extract the Authorization header and verify your JWT using a library like `jose`. Attach the decoded payload to ctx — or null if invalid. This gives every procedure access to `ctx.user` without repeated parsing.

For API key auth (e.g. server-to-server), follow the same pattern: parse `x-api-key`, look it up in your DB inside `createContext`, attach the result. Don't re-query per procedure.

Guard routes with a reusable `authedProcedure` built via `t.procedure.use(({ ctx, next }) => { ... })`. Throw `TRPCError({ code: 'UNAUTHORIZED' })` if `ctx.user` is null. Compose further: `adminProcedure = authedProcedure.use(requireRole('admin'))`.

For mTLS, termination happens at the proxy layer (nginx/APIM). Pass a verified-client header downstream; read it in `createContext` just like a JWT. tRPC itself is transport-agnostic — the chain stays identical regardless of how the token arrives.

bytes.topic: Pagination — cursor vs offset, total counts, infinite scroll patterns
bytes.title: tRPC Pagination — Cursor vs Offset, Total Counts, and Infinite Scroll
bytes.body: tRPC has no built-in pagination, so the pattern lives entirely in your router input/output schemas. Cursor-based pagination is the right default for feeds: define input with `z.object({ cursor: z.string().nullish(), limit: z.number().min(1).max(100).default(20) })`, return `{ items, nextCursor }` where `nextCursor` is the last item's ID (or null when exhausted). Offset pagination is simpler but breaks under concurrent inserts — reserve it for admin tables where total counts matter. Avoid `SELECT COUNT(*)` on every request; it's expensive. Instead, use a separate `getCount` procedure or return a `hasMore` boolean derived from fetching `limit + 1` items and slicing. On the frontend, use `useInfiniteQuery` from `@tanstack/react-query` with tRPC's `.useInfiniteQuery()` wrapper — pass `getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.nextCursor` and it wires cursor forwarding automatically. Flatten pages with `data.pages.flatMap(p => p.items)` for rendering. Avoid mixing cursor logic with `select` transforms, as they run post-fetch and can silently drop the `nextCursor` field.

bytes.topic: Filtering & Sorting — query param conventions, GraphQL args, protobuf fields
bytes.title: tRPC — Type-Safe Filtering & Sorting Without Query String Chaos
bytes.body:
tRPC procedures accept structured Zod-validated input objects, so filtering and sorting never devolve into stringly-typed query params. Define a reusable filter schema with `z.object()` and pass it directly to your `.input()` call — the client gets full autocomplete, the server gets runtime validation, no manual parsing.

For sorting, model direction as a Zod enum (`z.enum(['asc', 'desc'])`) and field as a string literal union. This mirrors what you'd express as GraphQL args or protobuf `OrderBy` fields, but with zero codegen — the schema IS the contract.

Pagination pairs naturally: use `cursor` + `limit` on the same input object. On the Prisma side, destructure directly into `findMany({ where, orderBy, take, cursor })`. tRPC's transformer layer (via `superjson`) handles Date objects in filters transparently, unlike REST query params where you'd coerce strings manually.

Avoid spreading filter state into individual procedure inputs — consolidate into a single `filters` sub-object using `z.object()` nesting so callers can partially override with `trpc.posts.list.useQuery({ filters: { status: 'published' } })`.

bytes.topic: Rate Limiting — per endpoint, per user, sliding window vs token bucket
bytes.title: tRPC Rate Limiting — Per-Endpoint, Per-User with Sliding Window vs Token Bucket
bytes.body:
tRPC's middleware layer is where rate limiting lives. Attach it per-procedure or at the router level using `t.middleware()`, giving you surgical control without touching business logic.

For per-user limits, key off `ctx.userId` (from your Clerk/session context). For per-endpoint, append the procedure path: `${ctx.userId}:${ctx.path}`. Use Upstash Redis's `@upstash/ratelimit` package — it supports both algorithms natively.

**Sliding window** resets the counter gradually over a rolling period. Better for bursty-but-fair clients — a user can't dump 100 requests at 11:59 PM and again at 12:00 AM. **Token bucket** replenishes at a fixed rate, allowing controlled bursts up to a max capacity. Prefer token bucket for AI inference procedures where you want to allow occasional spikes (e.g., 10 requests/min with burst of 3).

Attach strict sliding window to mutation procedures (writes, publishes), and token bucket to query procedures (search, feed). Throw `TRPCError({ code: 'TOO_MANY_REQUESTS' })` on limit breach — tRPC propagates this as HTTP 429 automatically when using `httpBatchLink`.

bytes.topic: API Versioning — URL vs header versioning, backward compatibility strategy
bytes.title: tRPC API Versioning — Router Namespacing Over URL Paths
bytes.body:
tRPC doesn't expose REST paths, so URL versioning (/v1/, /v2/) and header-based versioning are non-starters — the transport layer is HTTP but the contract is TypeScript. Version your API by namespacing routers instead.

Create sibling routers under your root appRouter: v1 and v2 sub-routers. Clients call trpc.v2.getUser.query() instead of trpc.v1.getUser.query(). Old procedures stay alive until clients migrate, giving you coexistence without HTTP gymnastics.

For backward compatibility within a version, lean on Zod's .optional() and .default() on inputs — add fields without breaking existing callers. For outputs, extend rather than mutate: add properties, never remove or rename. tRPC's end-to-end inference catches breakage at compile time, not runtime.

Deprecate gracefully with .meta({ deprecated: true }) and a custom middleware that logs or warns on deprecated calls. When a procedure is safe to remove, delete the router key — TypeScript will immediately surface every client call site that needs updating, making the migration surface area explicit and compiler-enforced.

bytes.topic: Testing — contract testing, mocking, integration tests per protocol
bytes.title: tRPC Testing — Contract, Mocking, and Integration Without REST Overhead
bytes.body:
tRPC's type-safe contract lives entirely in your router definition — so contract testing means asserting your router's inferred types and procedure signatures haven't regressed, not validating HTTP schemas. Use `createCallerFactory` for integration tests: it bypasses HTTP entirely, calling procedures directly with full type inference and real middleware execution. This lets you test auth guards, input validation via Zod, and business logic in one pass without spinning up a server.

For unit tests, mock the context object instead of the router itself. Inject a fake `ctx` with stubbed `db` or `user` values into `createCaller(ctx)` — this isolates procedure logic without touching transport. Avoid mocking the tRPC client in frontend tests; use `@trpc/server/adapters/fetch` with `msw` to intercept at the fetch layer instead, preserving the real client stack. For true contract regression, pair `zod` schema snapshots with `vitest` — serialize your input/output schemas and diff them across commits to catch breaking changes before they reach clients.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — consistent error format across protocols, problem+json
bytes.title: tRPC Error Handling — Consistent problem+json Across HTTP and WebSocket
bytes.body:
tRPC normalizes errors through a single `errorFormatter` — defined once in `initTRPC.create()`, it fires for every procedure failure across HTTP and WebSocket transports alike. Without it, clients receive tRPC's raw shape (`code`, `message`, `data`), which diverges from RFC 7807 problem+json expected by many API consumers.

The formatter receives `shape` (tRPC's normalized output) and `error` (the original `TRPCError` or wrapped cause). Inject a `problem` field into `shape.data` using `shape.data.httpStatus` for the status integer and `error.code` for the title. The `type` URI acts as a machine-readable error namespace — tRPC codes like `NOT_FOUND` and `BAD_REQUEST` map cleanly to unique URIs.

Because tRPC serializes the formatted shape over both HTTP (JSON body) and WebSocket (message envelope), you get one source of truth for error structure. Pair this with a `error.cause instanceof ZodError` check inside the formatter to surface field-level validation failures in the `detail` field without breaking the RFC 7807 contract.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — safe vs idempotent methods, idempotency keys for mutations
bytes.title: tRPC Idempotency — Safe Queries, Risky Mutations, and Idempotency Keys
bytes.body:
tRPC's `query` procedures are safe and idempotent by design — calling `trpc.user.getById.query(id)` ten times never mutates state and always returns the same result. Mutations are a different story: `trpc.order.create.mutate()` called twice creates two orders.

To make mutations idempotent, embed an `idempotencyKey` field in your Zod input schema. On the server, check a fast store (Redis or a DB unique constraint) before executing side effects. If the key exists, return the cached response. If not, execute and persist.

This matters most with tRPC's TanStack Query integration: failed mutations are retried automatically, and without idempotency guards, a network timeout followed by a retry can double-charge a customer or create duplicate records.

Generate keys client-side with `crypto.randomUUID()` scoped to the operation. Expire them after 24h — that window covers all realistic retry scenarios. Namespace keys by mutation type (`order:create`, `payment:capture`) to prevent cross-operation collisions. The server should treat the key as a content-addressed cache, not a lock.

bytes.topic: Security — input validation, CSRF, injection, GraphQL introspection risks
bytes.title: tRPC Security — Zod Boundaries, CSRF, and Procedure Exposure
bytes.body:
TypeScript types vanish at runtime. A tRPC procedure without `.input()` accepts arbitrary payloads — no type error, no warning. Every public and protected procedure must declare an explicit Zod schema via `.input()`; this is your only runtime validation boundary, and it's where injection is stopped.

CSRF is not handled automatically by tRPC's HTTP adapter. The `createHTTPHandler` and `fetchRequestHandler` don't restrict cross-origin requests unless you configure CORS explicitly. Pair the adapter with a CORS middleware that whitelists known origins, and for mutation-heavy APIs, validate the `Origin` or `Referer` header inside a reusable `t.middleware()`.

tRPC has no introspection endpoint like GraphQL, but your router shape leaks if you import server-side router types into client bundles — a common mistake when sharing a monorepo `AppRouter` type without tree-shaking boundaries. Export only the type (`export type AppRouter`), never the runtime router. Use `t.procedure.use(isAuthed)` on every non-public procedure; an unguarded `.query()` on a protected resource is invisible to tRPC's type system but fully exploitable at runtime.

bytes.topic: Caching — HTTP cache headers, CDN, ETag and conditional requests
bytes.title: tRPC Caching — HTTP Headers, ETags, and CDN-Safe Queries
bytes.body: tRPC queries default to POST, which CDNs and browsers refuse to cache. Switch to GET by setting `methodOverride: 'GET'` on `httpLink` — queries then hit `GET /api/trpc/users.list?input=...`, a URL CDNs can cache and browsers can revalidate. Add cache semantics server-side via `responseMeta` in your tRPC handler. This callback receives the resolved procedure `type` and `errors` array, letting you conditionally emit headers only for successful queries. For ETag-based conditional requests, hash the serialized response and return it as the `etag` header — clients send `If-None-Match` on repeat requests, enabling 304 short-circuits without re-fetching the database. Key constraint: `httpBatchLink` encodes multiple calls into one URL, breaking CDN cache key isolation. Use `splitLink` to route idempotent queries through `httpLink` with GET and all mutations through `httpBatchLink`. Pair `max-age` with `stale-while-revalidate` for zero-latency reads on stale-tolerant data — the CDN serves the cached byte instantly while revalidating in the background. ETags give you byte-exact freshness; SWR gives you perceived speed.

bytes.topic: Performance — payload size, compression, connection reuse, multiplexing
bytes.title: tRPC Performance — Batching, Streaming, and Payload Optimization
bytes.body:
tRPC's default setup fires one HTTP request per procedure call. Switching to `httpBatchLink` collapses simultaneous queries into a single POST, slashing round trips and connection overhead without any change to your procedure definitions.

For payload size, replace the default JSON transformer with `superjson`. It handles `Date`, `Map`, `Set`, and `undefined` natively with a compact wire format, and eliminates manual serialization glue on both router and client. Always select only the fields you need in your Prisma queries — returning raw model objects is the fastest way to bloat tRPC responses.

tRPC v11 ships `httpBatchStreamLink`, which streams each procedure's response as it resolves. A batch of three queries no longer blocks on the slowest — fast responses arrive immediately while slower ones continue in-flight.

Use `splitLink` to route subscriptions through `httpSubscriptionLink` (SSE) while queries and mutations use the batch-stream link. This keeps long-lived SSE connections out of the short-lived HTTP pool, eliminating head-of-line blocking across connection types.

Finally, add Express `compression()` middleware or set `compress: true` in your Next.js config — tRPC's JSON payloads compress 60–80% with gzip.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Protocol — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC decision framework
bytes.title: tRPC — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC: A Decision Framework for tRPC Users
bytes.body:
tRPC sidesteps the REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC debate for TypeScript monorepos — you get end-to-end type safety with zero schema duplication. But knowing when to step outside tRPC matters as systems grow.

Stay in tRPC when your client and server share a TypeScript codebase (Next.js, React + Node), you want automatic request batching via `httpBatchLink`, or you need subscriptions via `wsLink` without GraphQL's resolver overhead.

Reach for REST when building a public API consumed by non-TS clients or when you need HTTP caching semantics (ETags, CDN-friendly GETs). tRPC queries use GET by default, but the RPC shape breaks standard REST conventions that third-party consumers expect.

Reach for gRPC for high-throughput inter-service communication or bidirectional HTTP/2 streaming — tRPC subscriptions use WebSocket, not HTTP/2 streams, so latency-sensitive internal services benefit from gRPC's binary framing.

Skip GraphQL unless multiple independent consumer teams need client-driven query shaping. tRPC's `.select()` transformer achieves similar payload trimming without a resolver layer or schema file. Use `splitLink` to route tRPC procedures and legacy REST calls through the same client.
